business_quality.rationale: Backward-looking FY2025 segment data show a high-margin Search/advertising core (Google Services operating margin of roughly 40.7% on $342.7B of FY2025 revenue) funding a Cloud segment whose operating income more than doubled year over year and whose Q1 2026 reported margin nearly doubled to 32.9% from 17.8% a year earlier. This is a structurally strong, diversified business, but quality is tempered by binding antitrust remedies already entered against the core Search distribution model and by a revenue base still more than 70% dependent on advertising.

risk_profile.rationale: Risk is elevated by concrete, already-realized legal outcomes rather than speculative overhang: a final judgment with operative remedies was entered in the DOJ Search case in December 2025, and a mixed liability ruling against Google's publisher ad-tech tools was issued in April 2025 with a DOJ remedy proposal that the company itself states could have a material adverse effect on its business. Separately, 10-K risk factors flag power, water, and land constraints on technical infrastructure scaling, and capital expenditures are guided sharply higher into FY2026 and FY2027, increasing depreciation and capital-intensity risk.

expectations_gap.rationale: Consensus notes frame Cloud as a long-dated option not yet re-rated and antitrust as a generic valuation discount. Completed-period evidence suggests the margin inflection in Cloud is not purely prospective: FY2025 10-K segment data show Cloud operating income roughly doubling year over year, and the Q1 2026 earnings call reports realized (not guided) margin expansion to 32.9% and a sequential near-doubling of backlog to $462 billion. This is a plausible, evidence-grounded gap, though it relies partly on a single recently reported quarter.

variant_perception.rationale: The variant combines an audited FY2025 10-K backlog figure ($242.8 billion) with an unaudited, management-disclosed Q1 2026 backlog figure ($462 billion) to infer an accelerating bookings trend. The magnitude and characterization of the most recent backlog figure, and its conversion timeline, rest on management's own description in the earnings call rather than audited disclosure, which is a meaningful limitation on how much weight the variant can bear.

valuation.rationale: No reverse DCF or price target is produced per instructions. A Bear/Base/Bull scenario range is provided below, built on the same backlog-conversion, CapEx, and antitrust-remedy variables driving the rest of the analysis, without an implied recommendation.

catalyst.rationale: Near-term catalysts are binary and legal/operational rather than purely financial: appellate outcomes in the Search and ad-tech antitrust cases, the pace of FY2026 CapEx realization against the $180-190 billion guided range, and whether Cloud backlog-to-revenue conversion tracks the 'just over 50% within 24 months' figures disclosed in both the 10-K and the Q1 2026 call.

market_expectations: Per the supplied consensus notes, the market is pricing Alphabet at roughly $165 per share (~$2 trillion market cap), implying consensus FY2026 total revenue growth of approximately 11-12% with Google Cloud growing approximately 28-30%. The Street is described as valuing Alphabet primarily as a Search-dependent advertising business with Cloud treated as a long-dated option rather than a business already re-rating, and as treating the unresolved Search distribution and ad-tech antitrust cases as a valuation discount rather than an existential risk. A central debate flagged in consensus is whether the rise in CapEx (from $91.4 billion in FY2025 to a guided $180-190 billion in FY2026) compresses margins faster than Cloud revenue can inflect to absorb it.

observations.statement: Google Cloud's profitability inflection is visible in completed, reported results rather than only in forward guidance: FY2025 10-K segment data show Cloud operating income increasing $7.8 billion year over year on $15.5 billion of incremental revenue, and the Q1 2026 earnings call reports operating margin nearly doubling year over year to 32.9% from 17.8%, with operating income tripling to $6.6 billion in the quarter.

supporting_evidence.quote: Google Cloud operating income increased $7.8 billion from 2024 to 2025.
Google Cloud revenues increased $15.5 billion from 2024 to 2025, primarily driven by growth in Google Cloud Platform largely from infrastructure and platform services.
Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.

observations.statement: Cloud revenue backlog, a contractually grounded forward indicator rather than a pure guidance figure, was $242.8 billion as of the FY2025 10-K (December 31, 2025) and was reported as having nearly doubled sequentially to $462 billion by the end of Q1 2026, with both disclosures stating that just over 50% of backlog is expected to convert to revenue within 24 months.

supporting_evidence.quote: we had $242.8 billion of remaining performance obligations
We expect to recognize just over 50% of the revenue backlog as revenues over the next 24 months with the remainder to be recognized thereafter.
And Google Cloud's backlog nearly doubled sequentially, reaching $462 billion at the end of the first quarter.
just over half of it will convert to revenue in the next 24 months.

observations.statement: Capital expenditures are rising sharply on both a trailing and forward basis: FY2025 capex was $91.4 billion versus $52.5 billion in FY2024, the 10-K states the company expects to significantly increase 2026 infrastructure investment relative to 2025, and the Q1 2026 call disclosed a raised FY2026 CapEx guidance range of $180-190 billion plus an expectation that 2027 CapEx will increase further, alongside management's own statement that rising technical infrastructure investment will continue to pressure the P&L through higher depreciation and data center operating costs.

supporting_evidence.quote: During the years ended December 31, 2024 and 2025, we spent $52.5 billion and $91.4 billion on capital expenditures, respectively.
In 2026, we expect to significantly increase, relative to 2025, our investment in our technical infrastructure, including servers and network equipment, and data centers.

We are updating our full year 2026 CapEx guidance range to $180 billion to $190 billion, up from our previous estimate of $175 billion to $185 billion to now include investment related to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March.
we expect our 2027 CapEx to significantly increase compared to 2026.

the significant increase in our investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.

observations.statement: Antitrust risk has moved from a generic overhang to concrete, completed legal outcomes during the period covered by the 10-K: a final judgment with operative remedies was entered against Google in the DOJ Search distribution case in December 2025, and a separate April 2025 ruling in the advertising-technology case found Google's publisher tools anticompetitive, with the DOJ's pending remedy proposal described by the company as including structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on its business.

supporting_evidence.quote: In August 2024, the US District Court for the District of Columbia ruled against Google. A final judgment was entered in December 2025, which, among other things, imposes restrictions on how Google distributes its services and requires Google to share certain search data with and offer syndication services to certain competitors.

In April 2025, the US District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia issued a mixed decision in the DOJ case against Google, ruling that neither Google's advertiser tools nor the DoubleClick and AdMeld acquisitions were anticompetitive, but that Google's publisher tools unfairly excluded rivals.
The DOJ's remedy proposal includes structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on our business.
As of December 31, 2025, we had short-term accrued legal and regulatory fines and settlements of $15.6 billion.

observations.statement: The core Google Services segment remains a very high-margin base funding infrastructure investment: Google Services operating income increased $18.1 billion in FY2025, and the Q1 2026 call reported a 45.3% Google Services operating margin, even though the company still generated more than 70% of FY2025 total revenue from online advertising, leaving consolidated results concentrated in a single, regulator-targeted revenue stream.

supporting_evidence.quote: Google Services operating income increased $18.1 billion from 2024 to 2025.
Google Services operating income increased 24% to $40.6 billion and operating margin was 45.3%.
We generated more than 70% of total revenues from online advertising in 2025.

observations.statement: A meaningful share of reported net income growth in both the FY2025 10-K and the Q1 2026 call is attributable to non-operating, largely non-cash mark-to-market gains on non-marketable equity securities rather than core operating performance, which is a quality-of-earnings consideration for headline net income and EPS growth figures.

supporting_evidence.quote: OI&E of $29.8 billion for the year ended December 31, 2025 included net gains on equity securities of $24.1 billion, primarily related to unrealized gains on our non-marketable equity securities.
Other income and expenses was $37.7 billion, representing a meaningful increase from the prior year, primarily due to unrealized gains in our nonmarketable equity securities portfolio.
Net income increased 81% to $62.6 billion and earnings per share increased 82% to $5.11.

observations.statement: Management has stated that near-term Cloud growth is itself constrained by compute supply rather than demand, and the 10-K separately identifies power, water, and land availability as constraints on technical infrastructure scaling, both of which bear on how quickly reported backlog can convert to recognized revenue.

supporting_evidence.quote: we are compute constrained in the near term. And as an example, our cloud revenue would have been higher if you were able to meet the demand.
Our ability to scale our technical infrastructure is increasingly constrained by the availability of power, water, and land.

variant_hypothesis: Consensus, per the supplied notes, treats Google Cloud as a long-dated option within a Search-dependent advertising business and treats the antitrust cases mainly as a valuation discount. Completed-period evidence in the FY2025 10-K and the Q1 2026 earnings call suggests two things consensus may be underweighting in different directions: (1) Cloud's margin and backlog inflection is already present in reported, not merely guided, results -- operating margin and operating income growth are realized facts as of the most recent reported quarter, not promises -- which could mean the 28-30% Cloud growth consensus figure understates the segment's near-term trajectory if backlog conversion holds; and (2) the antitrust risk consensus treats as a generic discount has already crystallized into a binding final judgment with operative remedies in the Search case and a structural-remedy proposal pending in the ad-tech case, which is more concrete and nearer-term than a static valuation discount implies. The variant is therefore two-sided rather than purely bullish.
why_consensus_may_be_wrong: The observations above show that both halves of the consensus framing rely on treating already-completed events as if they were still prospective. Cloud's margin expansion to 32.9% and operating income tripling are reported Q1 2026 facts, not forecasts, while the FY2025 10-K's $242.8 billion backlog figure is an audited point-in-time disclosure that the Q1 2026 call shows growing rapidly. At the same time, the Search case final judgment and the ad-tech liability ruling are completed legal events with defined remedies or remedy proposals on the record, not merely a probability-weighted future risk. A consensus that prices Cloud as a future option and antitrust as a flat discount may be insufficiently differentiating between these two now-realized developments and could be mispricing the magnitude and timing of both, in offsetting directions.

adversarial_research.bull_case: Cloud's profitability inflection is already realized in completed, audited or reported periods, not merely guided: FY2025 segment operating income roughly doubled year over year, and Q1 2026 operating margin nearly doubled year over year to 32.9% with backlog nearly doubling sequentially to $462 billion. The high-margin Google Services segment continues to fund this investment, with Google Services operating margin at 45.3% in Q1 2026 and operating income up $18.1 billion in FY2025, giving the company internal capital to sustain elevated CapEx without external financing pressure in the near term.
adversarial_research.bear_case: CapEx is rising faster than free cash flow can absorb it on a trailing basis: Q1 2026 capex of $35.7 billion against free cash flow of only $10.1 billion for the quarter shows a materially tighter FCF margin than the FY2025 full-year relationship between $91.4 billion of capex and $164.7 billion of operating cash flow, and management itself states that rising technical infrastructure investment will continue to pressure the P&L through higher depreciation and data center operating costs. Simultaneously, antitrust risk is no longer speculative: a final judgment with operative remedies already constrains how Google distributes its services and requires data-sharing and syndication obligations in the Search case, while the ad-tech case carries a pending DOJ remedy proposal the company itself describes as including structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on its business.
adversarial_research.key_disagreement: Whether the already-realized Cloud margin and backlog inflection (evidenced in completed FY2025 and Q1 2026 reported results) is sufficient to offset the combination of accelerating, capex-driven depreciation pressure and antitrust remedies that have also already been legally realized rather than merely threatened.

evidence_for_variant_view.quote: Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.
And Google Cloud's backlog nearly doubled sequentially, reaching $462 billion at the end of the first quarter.
Google Cloud operating income increased $7.8 billion from 2024 to 2025.
we had $242.8 billion of remaining performance obligations

evidence_against_variant_view.quote: We are updating our full year 2026 CapEx guidance range to $180 billion to $190 billion, up from our previous estimate of $175 billion to $185 billion to now include investment related to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March.

the significant increase in our investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.

In August 2024, the US District Court for the District of Columbia ruled against Google. A final judgment was entered in December 2025, which, among other things, imposes restrictions on how Google distributes its services and requires Google to share certain search data with and offer syndication services to certain competitors.
The DOJ's remedy proposal includes structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on our business.
OI&E of $29.8 billion for the year ended December 31, 2025 included net gains on equity securities of $24.1 billion, primarily related to unrealized gains on our non-marketable equity securities.

adversarial_research.rebuttal: Even granting the bear case's capex and depreciation pressure, the Search case remedies disclosed in the 10-K are data-sharing and syndication obligations rather than a structural break-up, and the ad-tech remedies remain at the proposal stage pending final judgment, with Google's own disclosure indicating it intends to appeal adverse portions of both rulings. The gap between a single quarter's depressed free cash flow ($10.1 billion against $35.7 billion of capex) and the trailing-twelve-month figure ($64.4 billion of free cash flow) suggests the Q1 2026 FCF compression may partly reflect capex timing rather than a structural breakdown in cash generation, though this cannot be confirmed from the supplied excerpts alone.
disconfirming_evidence: A future completed quarter showing Google Cloud operating margin reversing or declining rather than continuing to expand would undermine the inflection thesis.

Backlog-to-revenue conversion materially undershooting the 'just over 50% within 24 months' figure disclosed in both the 10-K and the Q1 2026 call would weaken the variant's reliance on backlog as a leading indicator.

An appellate court reinstating or expanding structural remedies (for example, divestiture-type relief) in either the Search or ad-tech antitrust matters would invalidate the rebuttal that remedies to date are non-structural.

Free cash flow continuing to compress as a percentage of operating cash flow over multiple quarters, rather than recovering toward the FY2025 trailing-twelve-month relationship, would indicate the capex increase is structurally outpacing cash generation rather than being a timing effect.
adversarial_research.final_synthesis: The completed-period evidence supports a thesis that is bullish on Cloud's already-realized margin and backlog trajectory but acknowledges that antitrust risk has also moved from speculative to concrete during the same period, and that capex acceleration creates real near-term free cash flow pressure visible in the Q1 2026 print. The variant is best characterized as a belief that consensus has not fully priced the realized (not merely promised) nature of the Cloud inflection, while simultaneously not assuming away the now-binding nature of antitrust remedies, producing a constructive but not unconditional stance.

unknowns: Whether the $462 billion Q1 2026 backlog figure, which is management-disclosed in an earnings call rather than an audited filing, will be confirmed or restated in a subsequent 10-Q or 10-K, and whether its composition (GCP contracts versus TPU hardware agreements) shifts the conversion timeline described qualitatively as 'just over half... within 24 months.' Because this figure and its forward conversion characterization come from management's own description on the earnings call rather than from audited backward-looking disclosure, the variant hypothesis's reliance on it should be treated as a limitation.

The outcome of pending appeals in both the Search case (appealed by Google in January 2026, with the DOJ and state Attorneys General cross-appealing in February 2026) and the ad-tech case (where a final remedies judgment was still pending as of the FY2025 10-K), including whether any structural remedies are ultimately imposed.
Actual realized FY2026 capital expenditures relative to the guided $180-190 billion range, and the resulting trajectory of free cash flow beyond the single Q1 2026 data point.

The realized margin and integration effects of the Wiz and Intersect acquisitions on the Google Cloud segment, given the 10-K describes both as pending FY2026 closings while the Q1 2026 call describes Wiz as closed in March 2026 with a disclosed low single-digit percentage point margin headwind for the remainder of 2026.

The extent to which FY2025 and Q1 2026 net income and EPS growth are attributable to non-operating, non-cash gains on non-marketable equity securities versus core operating income growth, which the supplied excerpts do not allow to be fully decomposed on a comparable basis across periods.

top_risks: Antitrust remedies in the Search case (data-sharing and syndication obligations already entered) and a pending structural-remedy proposal in the ad-tech case represent realized and near-term legal risk rather than a distant or purely theoretical overhang.

Capital expenditure acceleration (FY2025 capex of $91.4 billion rising to a guided $180-190 billion in FY2026, with further increases expected in 2027) creates depreciation and free cash flow pressure that was already visible in Q1 2026's $10.1 billion of free cash flow against $35.7 billion of quarterly capex.

Compute, power, water, and land constraints disclosed in the 10-K and referenced directly by management as limiting near-term Cloud revenue could cap how quickly backlog converts to recognized revenue.

Reported net income and EPS growth include a substantial non-operating, largely non-cash component from unrealized gains on non-marketable equity securities, which introduces volatility and quality-of-earnings risk to headline growth figures.

Revenue remains concentrated, with more than 70% of FY2025 revenue derived from online advertising, leaving the business exposed to the outcome of the same antitrust matters affecting Search distribution and ad-tech practices.

bear.label: Bear
assumptions: Cloud backlog-to-revenue conversion runs slower than the 'just over 50% within 24 months' figure disclosed in both the 10-K and the Q1 2026 call, as compute, power, water, and land constraints noted in the 10-K limit deployment.

FY2026 capex lands at or above the top of the guided $180-190 billion range with 2027 capex increasing further as indicated on the Q1 2026 call, compressing free cash flow similarly to the Q1 2026 quarterly pattern of $35.7 billion of capex against $10.1 billion of free cash flow.

Antitrust remedies expand beyond the data-sharing and syndication obligations already entered in the Search case, and the ad-tech case remedy proceeding adopts some version of the DOJ's structural remedy proposal.
bear.implied_outcome: Operating margin expansion in Cloud stalls or reverses from the Q1 2026 level as infrastructure costs and depreciation outpace revenue conversion, while legal remedies impose additional structural costs or constraints on the Search distribution business, compressing consolidated free cash flow growth relative to the FY2025 trailing-twelve-month trend.

supporting_evidence.quote: We are updating our full year 2026 CapEx guidance range to $180 billion to $190 billion, up from our previous estimate of $175 billion to $185 billion to now include investment related to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March.

the significant increase in our investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.
Our ability to scale our technical infrastructure is increasingly constrained by the availability of power, water, and land.
The DOJ's remedy proposal includes structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on our business.
Free cash flow was $10.1 billion in the first quarter and $64.4 billion for the trailing 12 months.

base.label: Base
assumptions: Cloud backlog converts roughly in line with the disclosed 'just over 50% within 24 months' guidance, with operating margin holding near the 32.9% level reported in Q1 2026 rather than continuing to expand at the same rate.

FY2026 capex lands within the guided $180-190 billion range, with free cash flow recovering toward something closer to the FY2025 trailing-twelve-month relationship between operating cash flow and capex rather than remaining at the compressed Q1 2026 quarterly level.

The Search case remedies already entered (data-sharing and syndication obligations) stand through appeal without material expansion, and the ad-tech case resolves with a remedy short of full structural separation.
base.implied_outcome: Google Services continues to fund Cloud investment from a high-margin base while Cloud's profitability gradually consolidates near recently reported levels, consistent with a business executing on its full-stack AI infrastructure strategy without a major negative legal or capital-intensity surprise.

supporting_evidence.quote: We expect to recognize just over 50% of the revenue backlog as revenues over the next 24 months with the remainder to be recognized thereafter.
just over half of it will convert to revenue in the next 24 months.
Google Services operating income increased 24% to $40.6 billion and operating margin was 45.3%.
Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.
During the years ended December 31, 2024 and 2025, we spent $52.5 billion and $91.4 billion on capital expenditures, respectively.

bull.label: Bull
assumptions: Cloud backlog conversion and new bookings continue at a pace consistent with the sequential near-doubling reported between the FY2025 10-K backlog figure ($242.8 billion) and the Q1 2026 call figure ($462 billion), with operating margin continuing to expand from the 32.9% level reported in Q1 2026.

TPU hardware sales and enterprise AI solution revenue, described on the Q1 2026 call as growing nearly 800% year over year, scale further as compute constraints described by management ease over the guided capex cycle.
Antitrust remedies in both the Search and ad-tech cases are narrowed or overturned on appeal, limiting their effect on the Google Services revenue base.
bull.implied_outcome: Cloud's realized FY2025-Q1 2026 margin and backlog inflection continues, with the segment's profitability scaling toward Google Services-like margins over a multi-year horizon as the high-margin advertising base continues funding infrastructure investment with limited incremental legal constraint.

supporting_evidence.quote: And Google Cloud's backlog nearly doubled sequentially, reaching $462 billion at the end of the first quarter.
we had $242.8 billion of remaining performance obligations
Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.
In Q1, revenue from products built on our GenAI models grew nearly 800% year-over-year.
we are compute constrained in the near term. And as an example, our cloud revenue would have been higher if you were able to meet the demand.

green_flags.trigger: A subsequent quarterly or annual filing reports Google Cloud operating margin continuing to expand from the 32.9% level reported for Q1 2026, or backlog continuing to grow from the $462 billion figure disclosed on that call.
green_flags.rationale: Continued realized (not merely guided) margin and backlog expansion would strengthen the variant hypothesis that Cloud's profitability inflection is durable rather than a single-quarter event.

evidence.quote: Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.
And Google Cloud's backlog nearly doubled sequentially, reaching $462 billion at the end of the first quarter.

green_flags.trigger: Free cash flow recovers toward the FY2025 trailing-twelve-month relationship between operating cash flow ($164.7 billion) and capex ($91.4 billion), rather than remaining at the compressed Q1 2026 quarterly level.
green_flags.rationale: This would indicate the Q1 2026 free cash flow compression was a timing effect of capex acceleration rather than a structural breakdown in cash conversion.

evidence.quote: Free cash flow was $10.1 billion in the first quarter and $64.4 billion for the trailing 12 months.
CapEx was $35.7 billion in the first quarter with the overwhelming majority of the spend in technical infrastructure to support the AI opportunities we see across the company.

yellow_flags.trigger: FY2026 capex tracks toward the top of the guided $180-190 billion range, or 2027 capex guidance (flagged on the Q1 2026 call as expected to increase significantly versus 2026) is confirmed at a level well above current consensus.
yellow_flags.rationale: Capex at or above the high end of guidance increases depreciation and free cash flow pressure risk identified in both the bear case and the top risks section.

evidence.quote: We are updating our full year 2026 CapEx guidance range to $180 billion to $190 billion, up from our previous estimate of $175 billion to $185 billion to now include investment related to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March.
we expect our 2027 CapEx to significantly increase compared to 2026.

the significant increase in our investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.

yellow_flags.trigger: Backlog-to-revenue conversion in subsequent filings runs materially slower than the 'just over 50% within 24 months' figure disclosed in both the 10-K and the Q1 2026 call.
yellow_flags.rationale: Slower conversion would weaken the variant's reliance on backlog as a leading indicator of realized Cloud revenue growth.

evidence.quote: We expect to recognize just over 50% of the revenue backlog as revenues over the next 24 months with the remainder to be recognized thereafter.
just over half of it will convert to revenue in the next 24 months.

red_flags.trigger: An appellate court reinstates, expands, or adds structural remedies (such as divestiture-type relief) in either the Search distribution case or the advertising-technology case beyond the data-sharing and syndication obligations and pending remedy proposal already on the record.
red_flags.rationale: Structural remedies would represent a materially more severe and durable outcome than the data-sharing/syndication relief already entered, directly contradicting the rebuttal that remedies to date are non-structural.

evidence.quote: In August 2024, the US District Court for the District of Columbia ruled against Google. A final judgment was entered in December 2025, which, among other things, imposes restrictions on how Google distributes its services and requires Google to share certain search data with and offer syndication services to certain competitors.
The DOJ's remedy proposal includes structural remedies that could have a material adverse effect on our business.

red_flags.trigger: Google Cloud operating margin or operating income declines in a future completed quarter relative to the levels reported for Q1 2026.
red_flags.rationale: A reversal in realized (not guided) Cloud profitability would directly undermine the core evidence supporting the variant hypothesis's bullish component.

evidence.quote: Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased from 17.8% in the first quarter of last year to 32.9%.

recommended_next_step: Obtain Alphabet's Q2 2026 10-Q once filed to determine whether the $462 billion Q1 2026 backlog figure and 32.9% Cloud operating margin disclosed on the earnings call are confirmed in audited or reviewed form, and to update the free cash flow and capex trend beyond the single Q1 2026 data point before treating the variant hypothesis as more than provisional.
